summary: Big day for the SE BeltLine corridor with Reynoldstown finally seeing ground broken on that long-delayed project near the trail, plus a new food hall opening soon in East Lake. Meanwhile, TSA chaos at Hartsfield has ICE agents walking around the terminals, though travelers say they're not doing much.

topStory.headline: Reynoldstown's Long-Delayed BeltLine Project Finally Breaks Ground
topStory.summary: After nearly five years of an empty lot, townhomes and condos are finally rising at Chester Avenue and Fulton Terrace. The 85-unit development sits just a block from the popular Eastside Trail. Construction had been stalled since the original developer cleared the site, but Atlanta-based Embry Development stepped in to make it happen.
topStory.body:
You know that corner lot near the BeltLine in Reynoldstown that's been sitting empty forever? The one that made you wonder if Atlanta was collecting vacant lots like baseball cards? Well, good news: actual construction equipment has finally shown up.

Embry Development is moving forward with 85 residential units at 195 Chester Avenue, right where Chester meets Fulton Terrace. The site had been cleared by Toll Brothers way back in 2021, then sat there looking sad while the rest of Reynoldstown gentrified around it. Embry bought the project from Toll Brothers two years ago and inherited all the permits, which explains why things are finally happening.

The timing couldn't be better, honestly. With the Eastside Trail literally a block away and Memorial Drive continuing to transform, this corner was practically begging for some density. The project will include both townhomes and condos, giving people options beyond the typical single-family teardowns that have been popping up throughout the neighborhood. It's the kind of missing middle housing that Reynoldstown actually needs as it continues evolving from industrial past to walkable future.
topStory.imageAlt: Construction equipment at the Reynoldstown development site near the BeltLine

stories.headline: Decatur Gets 18 'Missing Middle' Cottages Under $700K
stories.summary: The Cottages at Midway project is bringing standalone houses around shared courtyards to unincorporated Decatur. With median home values hitting $700K+ in the area, this village-style development aims to offer more accessible ITP living. The soft launch starts next week for the 2.1-acre site on Midway Road.
stories.body:
Finally, someone's trying to solve the 'missing middle' puzzle in Decatur, where you can either buy a $1.2 million craftsman or rent an apartment, with not much in between. The Cottages at Midway is bringing 18 standalone houses to a couple of underused lots on Midway Road, designed around shared courtyards and green space.

The timing is smart given that Decatur's median home values have climbed north of $700,000. These aren't going to be bargains exactly, but they're aimed at being more accessible than the typical teardown-and-rebuild projects that have been dominating the market. The village-style layout is a nice change from the usual suburban-style subdivisions too.

The project sits in unincorporated DeKalb County near downtown Decatur, putting residents within walking distance of the square and MARTA. Construction is wrapping up now, with a soft launch starting next week to drum up interest. It's the kind of thoughtful infill development that could serve as a model for other ITP communities struggling with the affordability crunch.
stories.imageAlt: Site plan for Cottages at Midway development in Decatur

stories.headline: West End BeltLine Townhomes Start Taking Shape
stories.summary: The GW Parkside project at 600 Hopkins Street is finally showing some vertical progress. Ackerman & Co. is building 23 for-sale townhomes near the BeltLine, with the first seven units coming to market this spring. It's a rare opportunity for homeownership along the Southwest BeltLine corridor.
stories.body:
If you've been walking the Southwest BeltLine and wondering when that construction site at Hopkins Street would turn into actual buildings, your patience is about to pay off. The GW Parkside project is finally showing some real vertical progress, with the first phase of townhomes taking shape.

Ackerman & Co. started demolition a year ago for this 23-unit development, and they've been pretty methodical about the whole thing. The first seven properties should hit the market this spring, which is perfect timing for anyone who's been watching the area evolve. For-sale options are pretty rare along the Southwest BeltLine corridor, so this could fill a real gap in the market.

The location is solid too, right near Lee + White where Ackerman has been building out that whole office and food scene. It's one of those spots where you can actually see the neighborhood transforming in real time. The West End has been having a moment lately, and projects like this are part of what's making the area feel more connected to the broader BeltLine ecosystem.
stories.imageAlt: GW Parkside townhomes under construction in West End

stories.headline: Robert Montwaid's East Lake Food Hall Opens Mid-April
stories.summary: The creator of NYC's Gansevoort Market is bringing Vela Park Food Works to East Lake near the golf club. Six chef-driven concepts plus craft cocktails are coming to the 12-acre rental community. It's Montwaid's third Atlanta food destination, following his other local successes.
stories.body:
Robert Montwaid, the guy who basically invented the modern food hall with New York's Gansevoort Market, is opening his third Atlanta project, and this one's headed to East Lake. Vela Park Food Works is finishing construction now as the centerpiece of that new rental community near East Lake Golf Club.

The food hall will feature six different chef-driven concepts along with craft cocktails and plenty of outdoor dining space. Montwaid knows how to curate these things, and his previous Atlanta ventures have been hits, so expectations are pretty high. The mid-April opening puts it right in prime spring weather territory, which should be perfect for that outdoor component.

East Lake has been quietly building momentum as a food destination, and this could be the kind of anchor project that really puts it on the map. The location near the golf club gives it a different vibe from Montwaid's other projects, potentially drawing both neighborhood regulars and destination diners. Plus, the rental community aspect means there's a built-in customer base right on site.
stories.imageAlt: Interior rendering of Vela Park Food Works food hall

stories.headline: Mushi Ni Brings Asian Comfort Food to Inman Park
stories.summary: Chefs Michael Le and Tanya Jimenez opened their first restaurant today on Elizabeth Street, just off the Freedom Park Trail. The pop-up-turned-permanent spot serves Peking chicken, soft shell crab bao, and Thai tea martinis for lunch and dinner.
stories.body:
After making their mark on Atlanta's pop-up scene, chefs Michael Le and Tanya Jimenez have finally landed a permanent home for Mushi Ni, opening today in Inman Park. The restaurant sits on Elizabeth Street, perfectly positioned just off the Freedom Park Trail where it can catch both neighborhood foot traffic and trail users.

The menu leans into Asian-American comfort food with dishes like soft shell crab bao, chicken longanisa bao, and their signature Peking chicken platters that feed two for $25. They're also doing cocktails, including Thai tea martinis that sound like the perfect match for their flavor profile. Both Le and Jimenez have serious restaurant industry credentials, with Jimenez having worked for Gordon Ramsay in New York before moving to Atlanta.

Inman Park has been building quite the food scene lately, and Mushi Ni adds another layer to what's becoming a pretty compelling dining destination. The location right off the Freedom Park Trail is smart positioning, capturing both the neighborhood crowd and the growing number of people who use the trail system to get around ITP.
stories.imageAlt: Peking chicken dish at Mushi Ni restaurant

stories.headline: Lewis Barbecue Opens Upstairs Smokehouse Near BeltLine
stories.summary: The Austin pitmaster has brought his 22-foot smokers to Ansley Mall, creating what might be the fanciest BBQ engine room in Georgia. Six smokers run 24/7 in a loft overlooking the new pedestrian bridge to the BeltLine. The brisket is already drawing raves from local food writers.
stories.body:
John Lewis has created something pretty special at Ansley Mall, and it's not just the barbecue that's impressive. The pitmaster has installed six 22-foot smokers in a spacious loft with panoramic windows overlooking the new pedestrian bridge to the BeltLine. It looks more like the world's fanciest engine room than your typical BBQ joint.

The operation runs around the clock, with the smokers staggered in eight-hour shifts to maintain that factory rhythm that turns out what reviewers are calling some of the best brisket in Atlanta. Lewis helped weld the smokers himself out of 1,000-gallon propane tanks, so there's serious craftsmanship behind the operation. The Austin-by-way-of-Charleston pedigree doesn't hurt either.

The location behind The Cook's Warehouse puts it right in the middle of that Ansley Mall renaissance, with easy access to the BeltLine for people coming from Midtown or Virginia-Highland. It's the kind of destination spot that could draw people from across ITP, especially with those panoramic windows making the whole smoking process part of the show.
stories.imageAlt: BBQ platter at Lewis Barbecue with loaded sides

stories.headline: ICE Agents Walk Atlanta Airport, But Travelers Say They're Not Helping
stories.summary: Federal immigration officers are patrolling Hartsfield-Jackson as the TSA shortage continues, but passengers report they're mostly just standing around. The deployment comes as more than 40% of TSA workers have been calling out during the government shutdown.
stories.body:
The sight of armed ICE agents in tactical gear walking through Hartsfield-Jackson has become the new normal as the government shutdown drags on, but don't expect them to speed up your security line. Travelers are reporting that while the federal presence is definitely visible, the agents seem to be mostly observing rather than actively helping with the chaos.

The deployment comes as TSA faces massive staffing shortages, with more than 40% of Atlanta's workforce reportedly calling out. Monday saw four-hour wait times and travelers camping out overnight in security lines. ICE officials say the agents are there for general security support, but they're not doing immigration enforcement or helping with actual screenings.

It's a weird situation that perfectly captures the dysfunction of the shutdown. You've got one federal agency sending armed agents to help another federal agency that can't pay its workers, but nobody seems to have figured out what the helping actually involves. Mayor Dickens assured residents the ICE agents won't be doing immigration enforcement, but for a lot of travelers, the whole thing just adds to the surreal nature of trying to fly right now.
stories.imageAlt: ICE agents walking through airport terminal

stories.headline: Decatur's Complete Streets Makeover Nearly Finished
stories.summary: The West Howard Avenue project is wrapping up after nearly a decade of planning. The one-mile stretch now features a two-way cycle track with concrete barriers, new sidewalks, and traffic calming measures between downtown Decatur and East Lake MARTA.
stories.body:
After almost ten years of planning and community meetings, Decatur's big Complete Streets experiment on West Howard Avenue is finally coming together. The roughly one-mile stretch between downtown Decatur and East Lake MARTA station now features a proper two-way cycle track with concrete barriers, plus new sidewalks and traffic calming measures.

The transformation is pretty dramatic if you remember how West Howard used to be basically a high-speed cut-through for people trying to avoid surface streets. Now it's designed to actually serve the people who live and work along the corridor, not just pass through it. The cycle track runs most of the length of the project, giving cyclists a protected route between two major destinations.

The timing is perfect with East Lake continuing to develop and more people looking for car-free ways to get around. The project connects downtown Decatur to the MARTA station, which could be a game-changer for commuters. It's also a nice example of what Complete Streets can actually look like in practice, rather than just as a planning buzzword.
stories.imageAlt: New cycle track and sidewalks on West Howard Avenue in Decatur

stories.headline: City Council Advances ICE Restrictions as Fire Union Drama Continues
stories.summary: The Public Safety Committee unanimously moved forward resolutions to bar ICE detention facilities in city limits and require documentation of ICE activities during police operations. Meanwhile, firefighters packed the meeting to criticize Mayor Dickens for not signing their first-ever union contract.
stories.body:
Atlanta's City Council took a strong stance against ICE operations Monday, with the Public Safety Committee unanimously advancing two resolutions that would seriously limit immigration enforcement within city limits. One bars ICE detention facilities entirely, while the other requires Atlanta police to document any ICE agent activities during their operations.

The full council will vote on both measures April 20, and given the unanimous committee support, passage seems likely. It's a direct response to the Trump administration's immigration policies and the visible presence of ICE agents at Hartsfield-Jackson. The documentation requirement is particularly interesting because it creates accountability for any cooperation between local police and federal immigration enforcement.

But the ICE resolutions weren't the only drama at the meeting. Dozens of firefighters showed up to blast Mayor Dickens for refusing to sign the city's first-ever firefighter union contract, which the council already ratified back in April 2025. It's becoming a real political headache for the mayor, with public safety workers openly questioning his support for labor rights while he's simultaneously trying to position Atlanta as an immigrant-friendly city.
stories.imageAlt: Atlanta City Council meeting with firefighters in attendance

stories.headline: National Archives Brings Historic Documents to Atlanta
stories.summary: The Freedom Plane landed at Hartsfield-Jackson Monday carrying founding documents for America's 250th anniversary tour. An 1823 Declaration of Independence engraving and Senate markup of the Bill of Rights will be on free display at Atlanta History Center through April 12.
stories.body:
While one side of Hartsfield-Jackson was dealing with TSA chaos, the other side welcomed some pretty special cargo Monday afternoon. The National Archives' Freedom Plane touched down carrying historic founding documents as part of an eight-city tour celebrating America's 250th anniversary.

The cargo includes an 1823 engraving of the Declaration of Independence and a U.S. Senate markup of the Bill of Rights, among other documents. They'll be on display at the Atlanta History Center from March 27 through April 12, with free admission for anyone who pre-registers online. The center has already seen 12,000 people sign up, so weekdays might be your best bet for actually getting in.

The timing is perfect with spring break season and the general appetite for experiencing history in person rather than just reading about it online. Atlanta's role as a major transportation hub makes it a natural stop for this kind of traveling exhibition. Plus, the Atlanta History Center knows how to handle these big displays, so it should be worth the trip regardless of which documents speak to you most.
stories.imageAlt: Historic documents being unloaded from the National Archives Freedom Plane

stories.headline: Park Pride Conference Tackles Climate Change Through Green Spaces
stories.summary: The 25th annual Parks & Greenspace Conference brought hundreds to the Atlanta Botanical Garden to discuss resilient parks and communities. The Southeast's largest parks conference focused on building climate resilience, with Atlanta's urban heat island problem taking center stage.
stories.body:
Park Pride's 25th annual conference tackled one of Atlanta's most pressing challenges: how to build resilient parks and communities in the face of climate change. Hundreds of attendees from across the Southeast packed into the Atlanta Botanical Garden to share strategies and solutions.

The focus on climate resilience couldn't be more timely, especially for Atlanta neighborhoods dealing with urban heat islands. Communities that lack tree canopy are seeing dangerously high temperatures, making parks and green infrastructure literally life-saving resources. The conference highlighted programs like UrbanHeatATL, which is working with residents to build coalitions and engage property management companies about cooling solutions.

Atlanta calls itself the 'city in a forest,' but that nickname loses meaning in underserved communities where the tree canopy is sparse. The conference brought together people working on everything from community gardens to major park redesigns, all united by the understanding that green spaces aren't just nice amenities but essential infrastructure for surviving climate change.
stories.imageAlt: Attendees at the Parks & Greenspace Conference discussing climate resilience
